n: Galerias Romanas da Rua da Prata
hook: A flooded Roman cryptoporticus opened only a few days a year through a hole in the street
facts: Underground arched galleries from the 1st century, built when Lisbon was Roman Olisipo
A cryptoporticus that propped up buildings above on the soft riverside ground
Sits below the water table; an aquifer floods it for most of the year
City pumps it dry and opens it only a few days each April and September
w: Lift a manhole on Rua da Prata and Roman Olisipo opens beneath your feet: arched corridors built two thousand years ago to prop up the riverside city. An aquifer drowns them most of the year, so crews pump them dry and let visitors down for just a handful of days each April and September. Antiquity on a schedule.

hook: A roofless Gothic nave left as a deliberate scar of the 1755 earthquake
facts: Founded 1389 by the warrior-monk Nuno Alvares Pereira, once Lisbon's grandest church
Roof and vaults collapsed in the 1755 earthquake during All Saints' Day mass
Lisbon chose never to rebuild it, keeping the open sky as a memorial
Sacristy museum holds Peruvian child mummies and an Egyptian mummy
w: When the 1755 quake struck on All Saints' Day, the vaults came down on a church packed for mass. Lisbon never rebuilt it, leaving the Gothic arches open to the sky as a wound that never closed. Tucked in the sacristy, a tiny museum keeps two Peruvian child mummies and an Egyptian one. Skeleton arches over an empty nave.

hook: A church gutted by fire and left with its blackened, cracked columns on show
facts: A 1959 fire killed two firefighters and destroyed centuries of gilt and altars
Reopened in 1994 with the fire-scorched stone deliberately left unrestored
Ground zero for the 1506 Lisbon Massacre of New Christians outside its doors
A memorial in the square marks the hundreds killed in those three days
w: Fire gutted this church in 1959, killing two firefighters and consuming the gilt. Instead of polishing the damage away, Lisbon reopened it in 1994 with the columns left charred and cracked, raw stone glowing red. Outside these doors began the 1506 massacre that killed hundreds of New Christians in three days. Beauty out of scorch marks.

hook: A working hospital for broken dolls, run by the same family since 1830
facts: Opened 1830, said to be the world's oldest doll hospital still on its original spot
Began with Miss Carlota, who sewed cloth dolls and mended children's broken ones
Run today by the fourth generation of the founding family
Upstairs museum holds thousands of dolls and shelves of spare limbs and eyes
w: It started in 1830 when Miss Carlota, sewing cloth dolls on her stoop, began mending children's broken ones, playing doll doctor. Almost two centuries on, the fourth generation still works the same address, drawers of glass eyes and spare limbs on the walls. Upstairs, a museum of thousands of patients. Surgery for the loved and broken.

hook: A standing-room cherry-liqueur counter pouring the same drink since 1840
facts: Opened 1840 by a Galician named Espinheira, first shop in Lisbon to sell ginjinha
Serves sour-cherry ginja macerated in aguardente with sugar and cinnamon
A doorway-sized bar; drinkers spill out onto the square, glass in hand
Order it 'com elas' to get a boozy cherry left in the bottom of the cup
legend: Tradition credits a friar with discovering the recipe by steeping ginjas in spirit
w: Barely wider than its doorway, this counter has poured the same sour-cherry liqueur since 1840, when a Galician named Espinheira first sold ginjinha in Lisbon. Drinkers take a thimble glass onto the square, asking 'com elas' for a boozy cherry in the bottom. Two centuries, one sticky shot.

n: Reservatorio da Mae d'Agua das Amoreiras
hook: A cathedral-like water cistern at the end of an 18th-century aqueduct
facts: Designed 1746 by Carlos Mardel, finished 1834, end point of the Aguas Livres Aqueduct
A vaulted stone chamber holding a still, dark pool fed from the hills
Stairs climb to a rooftop terrace over the Amoreiras skyline
Now part of the city's Water Museum and used for exhibitions and concerts
legend: They say killer Diogo Alves stole the keys here and hurled victims off the aqueduct
w: Water that crossed the hills on the great Aguas Livres Aqueduct ends in this vaulted stone hall, a still black pool under cathedral arches finished in 1834. Climb to the roof for the skyline. They say the killer Diogo Alves stole keys here and threw robbed victims off the aqueduct's parapet. A reservoir with a body count in its folklore.

hook: A former secret-police prison turned museum of resistance and isolation cells
facts: Housed in a 16th-century jail built over Roman remains; name from Arabic for dungeon
Held political prisoners of the Salazar dictatorship from 1928 to 1965
Around 30,000 prisoners passed through its cells in those years
Opened as a museum on 25 April 2015, anniversary of the Carnation Revolution
w: Its name comes from the Arabic for dungeon, and the building earned it: a 16th-century jail turned into the dictatorship's political prison, where some 30,000 passed through between 1928 and 1965. Stand inside the windowless isolation cells, barely a body wide. It opened on the revolution's anniversary. Memory in the dark.

hook: A 40-foot stone pyramid said to be Europe's largest private mausoleum
facts: Built 1849 for the Dukes of Palmela in the Prazeres Cemetery
A cubic crypt under a pyramid, room for around 200 of the family dead
Designed by Italian architect Giuseppe Cinatti with sculpture inside
Prazeres itself opened in 1833 after a cholera epidemic swept the city
w: Down an avenue of cypresses rises a stone pyramid forty feet high, often called Europe's largest private tomb. Built in 1849 for the Dukes of Palmela, its cubic crypt holds room for two hundred of the family dead. Beyond it stretch streets of mini-temples, a whole silent city laid out after the cholera of 1833. Death dressed as Egypt.

hook: A pharmacy museum of stuffed crocodiles, pickled seahorses and a Macau apothecary
facts: Opened 1996 in the National Association of Pharmacies building above the Tagus
Over 15,000 objects spanning ancient apothecaries to a NASA medical kit
Reconstructed shops include the only genuine Chinese pharmacy shown in the West
Cabinets of stuffed crocodiles, cobras, pufferfish and jars of pickled seahorses
w: Fifteen thousand objects trace how people tried to cure themselves, from old apothecaries to a NASA flight medical kit. Whole shopfronts stand reconstructed, including a Macau pharmacy billed as the only real Chinese one in the West. Look up for stuffed crocodiles and jars of pickled seahorses once sold as cures. Medicine's odd attic.

hook: A palace garden overrun by giant ceramic snakes, frogs and beetles
facts: Outdoor gallery of oversized faience creatures by the Bordalo Pinheiro factory
Snakes, lizards, toads, snails and beetles climb walls and lurk in the bushes
Set in the gardens of the 18th-century Palacio Pimenta, now the Museu de Lisboa
Project that helped pull the century-old ceramics factory back from bankruptcy
w: In the grounds of an 18th-century summer palace, giant glazed creatures have taken over: snakes down the walls, a colossal toad, beetles and snails the size of footstools, all in the lurid faience of the Bordalo Pinheiro works. The display also rescued the old factory from ruin. A menagerie that bites only in ceramic.

hook: A Renaissance mansion clad in over a thousand diamond-pointed stones
facts: Built 1523 for Bras de Albuquerque, son of the conqueror of Goa
Facade bristles with more than a thousand carved diamond spikes
Top floors fell in 1755 and were rebuilt from old tile panels and engravings
Now home to the Jose Saramago Foundation, with the writer's ashes outside
w: Over a thousand diamond-pointed stones bristle across this 1523 mansion, built for the son of the man who took Goa and modelled on a spiky palace in Ferrara. The 1755 quake sheared off its upper floors, later rebuilt from old tile panels. Nobel laureate Jose Saramago's ashes rest under an olive tree out front. A house in armour.

hook: A domed church so slow to build it became a Portuguese byword for endless work
facts: Begun in the 1680s, the great dome was only finished in 1966
Three centuries of delay gave Portuguese the phrase 'obras de Santa Engracia'
Converted into the National Pantheon, resting place of national figures
Holds the tomb of fado singer Amalia Rodrigues and cenotaphs of explorers
legend: Legend blames the delays on a curse laid by a man wrongly executed for sacrilege
w: Builders broke ground in the 1680s and capped the dome only in 1966, a delay so legendary the Portuguese still call any endless task 'obras de Santa Engracia.' Legend blames a curse from a man wrongly executed for sacrilege. Now the National Pantheon, its marble holds explorers and fado queen Amalia Rodrigues. Three centuries for a roof.
